title: Volary: The memory layer for the agentic era
speaker: Volary
summary: Peter Ebdenden presents Volary, a new memory layer for AI agents designed to address the challenges of building autonomous agents. He explains that Volary functions as an 'artificial hippocampus,' capturing and distilling episodic memory from agent interactions to help agents learn and improve over time. Unlike traditional memory products that rely on semantic memory and SDK integration, Volary focuses on episodic memory and works as a pass-through layer, making it compatible with agents you don't control. The presentation highlights how Volary helps agents build memories, become better attuned to user tasks, and reduces the need for manual prompt engineering.
topics: Volary: Memory layer for AI agents
quotes: Volary is the missing hippocampus for agents.
We preserve salient episodes across disparate agent conversations.
Volary combines episodic memory with deep reflection.
It sits between the agent and the LLM, observing the whole conversation.

Visual aids include diagrams illustrating how AI-generated code can be exploited, as well as a framework for managing AI security risks.

title: Forget the AI hype. Here's how real AI agents are built to solve real problems

title: Securing Your Software Supply Chain: Why SBOMs Are Essential for DevOps and Compliance
speaker: Unknown
summary: The speaker introduces 'S-bombs' — an informal term for 'Software Bill of Materials' (SBOMs) — as a critical tool for software transparency and compliance. The speaker shares their personal journey into this topic, driven by real customer requests and involvement in initiatives like 'Secure by Design' in the UK and US. They highlight the growing regulatory momentum behind SBOMs, citing the US executive order mandating them for government contracts, the EU’s CRA (Computer Security Act) coming into effect in December, and expectations that frameworks like PCI DSS 4.0, ISO, and NIST will soon require SBOMs. The speaker emphasizes that while the term 'S-bombs' is unsexy, the underlying concept is vital for modern software supply chain security.

title: The Psychology of Prompt Injection: AI’s Social Engineering Problem
speaker: Unknown
summary: The video presentation by Joe Bollen explores the psychological aspects of prompt injection in AI models, particularly focusing on how AI systems can be manipulated through social engineering tactics. The talk delves into the vulnerabilities of Large Language Models (LLMs) such as GPT-4o, Llama-3.2-3b, and Granite-3.1-8b, examining how attackers can exploit the models by using techniques like appealing to authority, urgency, empathy, and reciprocity. The presentation highlights that while traditional security vulnerabilities are deterministic, AI models introduce non-deterministic outcomes, making them more unpredictable and harder to secure. The speaker uses real-world examples and code snippets to illustrate how prompt injection can lead to unintended or harmful outputs, emphasizing the need for better safeguards and a deeper understanding of AI behavior.

title: Patrick Hyland @ Domino's - Pizza Platforms, Dynamic Capabilities and SRE/GenAI
speaker: Patrick Hyland
summary: Patrick Hyland, Senior Engineering Manager at Domino's, presents on the modernization of the company's technical capabilities, focusing on the evolution of its Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) function. He outlines how Domino's transitioned from a monolithic, legacy system to a more modern, dynamic platform supporting over 4 million pizzas sold annually. The talk details the challenges of the old system, including lack of developer ownership and reactive incident response, and introduces the new 'Phoenix' architecture as a strategic shift. He emphasizes the importance of 'Dynamic Capabilities'—a framework for adaptability, learning, and reconfiguration—enabling the business to respond to market changes and technological advancements. The presentation uses diagrams to illustrate the new architecture, including frontend and backend components, and the integration of DevOps practices.

We use an 'Anatomy of Capability' approach, and a reconfigurable Systems model.
Dynamic Capabilities are the key to give competitive advantage.

title: Raul Rodriguez @ Zühlke Engineering - Preventing Deaths: DevOps' 10,000 Success
speaker: Raul Rodriguez
summary: The video features Raúl Rodríguez, a DevOps engineer, presenting at the 'DevOps Exchange London' event. He discusses the critical role of DevOps in preventing COVID-19 deaths through the NHS app, highlighting how the app's digital contact tracing system helped save an estimated 9,600 lives. He also explores the broader DevOps philosophy, emphasizing collaboration across disciplines including policymakers, researchers, engineers, and security experts. The presentation includes visual aids such as a diagram of the NHS app's system architecture, a Venn diagram illustrating the roles in DevOps, and slides comparing the app's features to microservices. The video concludes with a thought-provoking question: if you had to save 9,600 lives in 12 weeks, what would you do? The speaker's message is clear: DevOps is not just a technical practice but a collaborative, human-centered approach to solving complex problems.

Preventing deaths: DevOps' 9,600 success.

title: Ben Stanley @ Temrel - Self-hosting LLMs for Dummies
speaker: Ben Stanley
summary: The video presentation by Ben Stanley explores the concept of self-hosting large language models (LLMs) for businesses, contrasting it with using third-party APIs like ChatGPT. Stanley explains that while using third-party APIs is the easiest way to interact with LLMs, it lacks privacy, as the data is sent to external services. He argues that for companies needing to keep their data private and tailor models to their specific use cases, self-hosting is the solution. The presentation outlines the three main components of an LLM pipeline: fine-tuning, streaming, and inference. It also discusses the technical requirements for self-hosting, including the need for powerful GPUs, such as NVIDIA A100s, to handle the memory demands of large models like LLaMA-2-70B and Falcon-40B. Stanley emphasizes that self-hosting allows organizations to maintain control over their data and create models that are fine-tuned to their specific needs, offering a more secure and customized alternative to public APIs.
quotes: The easiest way to use LLMs is via a 3rd-party API, e.g., ChatGPT.
But that's not private.
It's also not fine-tuned.
To get private, fine-tuned, up-to-date GenAI, we need to roll our own LLMs.
LLMs require so much compute, they don't have this flexibility.

title: Denys Vasyliev @ GfK - AIRe - AI Reliability Engineering
speaker: Denys Vasyliev
summary: The speaker, Denys Vasyliiev, presents on 'AI Reliability Engineering (AIRE)' at a DevOps Exchange event in London. He argues that AI is not just a fleeting trend but a significant technological advancement that will reshape industries. The talk covers the current state of AI, particularly focusing on the rapid growth of ChatGPT and LangChain, and highlights the challenges of deploying AI in production. Vasyliiev emphasizes the need for a new discipline—AI Reliability Engineering—to bridge the gap between ML Ops and SRE, addressing issues like model drift, data poisoning, and the complexity of managing AI systems at scale. He uses a demo to illustrate how an AI agent can autonomously perform tasks such as web searches, generating summaries, and deploying code, while also pointing out the potential pitfalls and the need for robust observability and monitoring.

AIRE: Bridging ML Ops and SRE

title: George Mamalakis @ META - Disaster Recovery @ Meta
speaker: George Mamalakis
summary: George Mama Likes, a Meta production engineer, discusses how the company handles data center disasters, using Hurricane Sandy in 2012 as a pivotal case study. At that time, Meta’s reliance on a single data center in Forest City meant that any failure could bring down the entire Facebook network. This crisis prompted the creation of a disaster recovery program focused on rerouting traffic globally during outages — a process Meta calls 'drain.' However, the complexity of rerouting traffic across thousands of microservices, each with unique dependencies and hardware configurations, made the solution far from simple. The presentation outlines the challenges of scale, service heterogeneity, and data locality, emphasizing the need for a platform that can intelligently manage traffic and service availability without duplicating resources or violating privacy laws. The goal is to ensure Facebook remains operational even when a data center is compromised.
quotes: If either one of them got down the whole Facebook network would be down.
This redirection of traffic from one data center to the rest of them is what we at Meta call a drain.
Well short answer is no it's not that simple because as we said the application server talks to various microservices which have their own dependencies.
The main challenges that we had here was first of all that not all of our data centers have the same hardware.
We don't need it to be everywhere and it is a huge volume of data and we would have some issues also with local laws maybe for privacy.

title: Damon Wright @ Capgemini - Kubernetes pods: they really are a container?
speaker: Damon Wright
summary: The video features Damon Wright, a Platform Engineer at Capgemini, delivering a presentation at the DevOps Exchange London event. The talk, titled 'Kubernetes Pods: They Really Are a Container?', delves into the technical underpinnings of Kubernetes pods, challenging the common misconception that a pod is simply a container. Wright explains that a pod is actually a collection of containers, and that the true essence of a container lies in Linux namespaces and control groups (cgroups). He uses terminal commands and slides to illustrate how containers are isolated using namespaces for filesystem, network, user, process, and time, and how cgroups manage resource allocation and fair usage. The presentation aims to demystify the difference between a pod and a container, providing a deeper understanding of how Kubernetes orchestrates containerized applications.
quotes: Kubernetes pods: they really are a container?
A container is two things: a collection of namespaces... and cgroups to enforce fair usage sharing.
At the heart of it, a container is two things: a collection of namespaces (at minimum chroot) and cgroups.
A pod is not a container. A pod is a collection of containers.
We're not talking about a single container. We're talking about a collection of containers.

title: Matteo Rinaudo @ AWS - "AWS CloudFormation - technical introduction"
speaker: Matteo Rinaudo
summary: The video presentation, delivered by AWS senior developer advocate Kai Landy, introduces AWS CloudFormation as a service for managing infrastructure as code. The session covers the core concepts of Infrastructure as Code (IaC), the benefits of describing infrastructure programmatically, and how CloudFormation enables developers to model, provision, and manage AWS resources. The speaker emphasizes best practices such as testing early, using lifecycle and ownership criteria, and validating code with tools like cfn-lint and TaskCat. The presentation also walks through the workflow: writing templates (YAML or JSON), uploading them to an S3 bucket, and deploying stacks. Visuals include slides detailing CloudFormation's structure, its historical context (announced February 25, 2011), and examples of template sections like Parameters, Resources, and Outputs. The session concludes with a focus on extending CloudFormation with community modules and hooks.

title: Sal Furino - "9 Things You Should Do When Starting to Use SLOs"
speaker: Sal Furino
summary: The video presentation by Kai Landy, a Site Reliability Engineer, discusses best practices for implementing Service Level Objectives (SLOs) in organizations. Landy emphasizes that while SRE books provide foundational knowledge, they often lack specific guidance on implementation. He highlights common pitfalls, such as teams attempting to build their own SRE paths without a clear framework, leading to inconsistent results. Landy advocates for a more structured approach to SLOs, focusing on how to use them effectively to drive reliability culture and decision-making. He also addresses misconceptions, such as the idea that SLOs aren't a culture change, arguing instead that they represent a significant shift in how organizations approach reliability. The presentation includes slides with key takeaways, such as 'Don’t Set and Forget' vs. 'Do Codify Your Reliability Targets,' 'Don’t say SLOs are Only For Engineers' vs. 'Do Different Groups Take Different Actions,' and 'Don’t Measure CPU Utilization' vs. 'Do Shine Light on Customer Journeys.'

Don’t Set and Forget. Do Codify Your Reliability Targets.

Don’t Measure CPU Utilization. Do Shine Light on Customer Journeys.
SLOs are a change in adopting a decision-making process across your organization towards reliability.

title: Kosseila Hd - "What’s OIDC and why should you use it asap in your GitHub pipeline"
speaker: Kosseila Hd
summary: The video presentation by Kosseila Haddadlene, a Solutions Architect at Eclipsys, explores the origins, workflow, and practical implementation of OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) in modern authentication, particularly within GitHub Actions. The speaker begins by contrasting European corporate communication styles with Canadian pragmatism, using it as a metaphor for technical explanations. The presentation then delves into the core concepts of OAuth 2.0, including its terminology (Client ID, Client Secret, Redirect URI) and workflow, before explaining how OIDC builds upon OAuth 2.0 to provide authentication and user information. Real-world examples are provided, including OIDC implementation in GitHub Actions, Azure, and AWS, with a focus on how these technologies enable secure, delegated access without requiring direct credential handling. The presentation concludes with a brief discussion on the risks of credential leakage, referencing a 2022 statistic showing 10 million GitHub credentials were exposed.

title: Tim Murphy @ ActionIQ - "Our Jenkins Journey: Lessons Learned"

title: Michael Liberman @ Kusari - Insights into the Software Supply Chain: An Introduction to GUAC
speaker: Michael Liberman
summary: Mike Lieberman, co-founder of Kusari and active in the open source community, introduces the concept of software supply chain security (SSCS) during a conference talk. He highlights the growing need for visibility and control over software components as they move from developer to production, emphasizing the risks introduced by third-party dependencies. He references real-world incidents like the Log4j vulnerability to illustrate how complex dependency chains can hide critical security flaws. The core problem, he explains, is managing the vast, often opaque, ecosystem of packages — many of which are externally sourced — and ensuring that every component is verified, licensed, and secure. He underscores the need for better data and visibility to track origins, authors, and integrity of code throughout the supply chain.
quotes: Software supply chain security is just STLC security. How are you securing from developer to production?

You're using Java, you rely on Spring, you rely on Log4j. Great. And the problem is like you think you just have a couple of layers of that, but you actually have a ton.
We need to know what's living where. We need to know who wrote the code. We need to know where we downloaded it from.
Which one of these dots is malware, right. And which one of these dots has like a license that we shouldn't be using and we could get sued over.

title: Tina Tyavlovskaya and Kabir Faisal @ ActionIQ - Rocky Journey to Replace Legacy Automation

summary: The video presents a case study on the migration of legacy test automation from Selenium to Cypress, highlighting the challenges and benefits of the transition. The speaker, Tina from ActionIQ, introduces the team and the project's context, emphasizing the need to replace outdated automation tools to improve efficiency and maintainability. The presentation details the journey of modernizing test cases, including the use of data-driven approaches, mocking data, and custom controls to ensure code reusability and stability. The team's experience includes conducting research, selecting a small set of test cases for validation, and executing parallel runs to compare legacy and new automation solutions. The results show significant improvements, such as a reduction in test execution time from 2-6 hours to 6-20 minutes, and a dramatic decrease in the defect rate from 40% to 5%. The presentation also outlines the key indicators of legacy automation issues, such as long feedback loops and inadequate coverage, and how the new approach addresses these challenges. title: Shirisha Vivekanand @ AWS - Building Resilience through Chaos Engineering
speaker: Shirisha Vivekanand
summary: The video presentation, delivered by Shirisha Vivekanandan, explores the concept of Chaos Engineering as a method for building system resilience. It begins by defining resilience as the ability of a system to recover from failure, emphasizing that failure is inevitable and should be embraced. The speaker outlines the AWS Well-Architected Framework, particularly focusing on the 'Reliability' pillar, which guides the design of resilient systems. The presentation then delves into the principles of Chaos Engineering, explaining how to systematically test systems by intentionally injecting failures to uncover weaknesses. The speaker highlights tools like AWS Fault Injection Simulator and discusses the importance of running tests regularly in pre-production and production environments. The talk also touches on the broader context of the Well-Architected Framework's other pillars, such as Operational Excellence, Security, Performance Efficiency, Cost Optimization, and Sustainability, to provide a holistic view of building robust systems. The presentation concludes by reinforcing that Chaos Engineering is not just about breaking things, but about understanding how systems behave under stress to ensure they can continue to operate as expected.

Joseph explains how Kubernetes’s autoscaling and node draining can inadvertently kill game servers and why this is unacceptable in real-time gaming contexts.

OEC is presented as a middleware-friendly, modern alternative to Express, despite being unofficially ported and lacking official support for other popular frameworks like Express or Koa. summary: The speaker shares their volunteer experience at Alastria’s core team, a nonprofit in Spain promoting the digital economy with over 500 European members. The talk emphasizes that there’s no one-size-fits-all solution in blockchain monitoring, and instead, teams must adapt using methods like web sockets, scraping, or forwarding. title: DevOps Exchange - Paul Sarbinowski, Google

Capitan enables templating infrastructure resources for cloud deployment using four input types: JSON, Gen.2, Helm charts, and Python. It generates output files in YAML, JSON, or TXT formats compatible with tools like Kubernetes, Terraform, Spinnaker, Prometheus, and Ansible. The tool also supports generating playbooks and documentation, and features a hierarchical inventory for deduplicating environment-specific data across targets. A key innovation is its automatic secret management, integrating with cloud KMS backends (GCP, AWS, etc.) to encrypt secrets in source control and decrypt them at deployment time. The philosophy behind Capitan is to abstract operational complexity and enable infrastructure as code by scripting deployment logic, without managing deployment mechanics (like Terraform apply or Kubernetes apply). The tool is designed for environments where version-controlled, reproducible infrastructure deployment is critical, and it allows embedding business logic into infrastructure definitions.

title: Slava Kalmykov @ Spotify - Continuous Delivery at Spotify

title: Ionut Craciunescu @ Wealth Wizards  Unblocking the release train with ISTIO Traffic Management

To solve this, they explored alternatives like feature flags and dedicated environments but found them too cumbersome or costly. Instead, they turned to Istio, a service mesh, which allows them to deploy changes independently by injecting sidecars into pods, enabling traffic routing and control without affecting the entire system. This approach lets each change proceed at its own pace, improving deployment flexibility while maintaining compliance.

He shares his background as one of Tandem’s earliest developers and his journey into DevOps, emphasizing the philosophy that doing the right thing should be simple and that not everything needs to change. The talk focuses on Tandem’s challenge to migrate from on-premises to AWS while simultaneously building a reliable, secure, and fast deployment pipeline. Matt highlights the pain points of legacy infrastructure management—especially the cumbersome change request process—and explains how Tandem leveraged existing tools like Terraform, start-hook, and DNS control to streamline operations. He ends with a light-hearted reflection on the value of simplicity and the importance of reacting quickly to security or feature needs in a highly regulated financial environment.

The goal is to relieve the administrative burden on freelancers and small business owners. Alistair reflects on the early days of the startup, starting in May 2017, and the pressure of building a regulated fintech product by January 2018. He describes the anxiety of making high-stakes decisions in a heavily regulated space and how the team chose to partner with a banking-as-a-service provider to avoid the complexities of owning card issuers or clearing houses directly. He also discusses architectural decisions, including opting for a relational database over NoSQL, and the need to handle asynchronous workloads like onboarding and tax calculations efficiently.

title: Jez McKingley - Refactoring in Ansible #DOXMOUTH
speaker: Jez McKingley
summary: The speaker presents a retrospective on how Antiball (likely intended to be Ansible) has evolved over the years, particularly focusing on the use of includes and refactoring code to improve maintainability. They work for a company that develops console-based business management software for agriculture and brown-head dealers, with around 480 dealerships across the region. The application is written in a language called 'Divell' (likely a typo or misstatement for 'Dell' or another tech term) and is console-based, with a Windows version. The speaker acknowledges that while their approach is functional, it may not be optimal, as they learned through documentation and Stack Overflow, and there’s limited internal expertise in CMDB (Configuration Management Database) to bounce ideas off. They explain three types of includes in Ansible: plain include, include_tasks, and include_role. The speaker emphasizes Ansible’s declarative nature and agentless architecture, which allows for pushing updates without waiting for agent feedback. They describe the building blocks: tasks (declarative goals), roles (grouped tasks), playbooks (collections of roles applied to hosts/groups), and inventory (host definitions and variables). The presentation is delivered in a casual, self-deprecating tone, admitting the code might be suboptimal but functional, and open to feedback.

title: Andrei Butnaru - Security and Automation - DOXMOUTH

The speaker emphasizes the challenges of innovation in complex, multi-component products and how automation is key to managing security efficiently. They describe integrating security testing — including OWASP ZAP scans — into continuous integration pipelines, enabling early detection of vulnerabilities without burdening human teams. Automation is portrayed as a necessary and effective response to the high cost and skill shortage in security roles, allowing teams to focus on high-value tasks while minimizing human error.

These systems, built on outdated tech like COBOL and running on hardware no longer available, were unsustainable and contributed to a developer exodus. Clark emphasizes the urgency of modernization and the shift toward a product-based, DevOps-driven approach to tackle business risk and innovation.

Dave notes that while Kubernetes offers a great platform and developer experience — especially for 'hello world' use cases — the team found gaps in native AWS integration, such as IAM, ALB support, and multi-account management, which they had previously solved with custom tools. The transition was strategic, not forced, and the team leveraged existing container experience with ECS before adopting Kubernetes as the next logical step in their platform evolution.

title: Tass Skoudros - Security as a shared responsibility - Managing Director - Servana

Goudra advocates for embedding security into the core of agile development workflows, making it a shared responsibility among all team members — from product owners to DevOps engineers. He argues that security cannot be outsourced effectively and must be integrated from the start of any infrastructure or feature development. title: Tom Wilkie @ Grafana Labs - Monitoring Kubernetes with Prometheus
speaker: Tom Wilkie
summary: Tom Wilkie, VP Product at Confiant Labs, kicks off his DevOps Exchange talk with a light-hearted introduction, acknowledging his new role and modest social media growth. He sets the stage by polling the audience on their use of Kubernetes and Prometheus, revealing that most attendees are familiar with both. The talk then dives into what Prometheus is — an open-source monitoring system inspired by Google’s Borgmon and developed by SoundCloud — emphasizing its text-based metrics, multi-dimensional data model, and powerful query language. Tom highlights Prometheus’s strengths in fast-moving, continuously deployed environments, thanks to its service discovery capabilities, while clarifying it’s not suited for logs or events. He briefly touches on the architecture of Prometheus, including its server, integrations, and ecosystem components, before transitioning into how it combines with Kubernetes for powerful observability use cases.
quotes: Who here is using Kubernetes? Hands up if you're using Kubernetes.

Prometheus is really good if you've got fast moving infrastructure.

title: Iain Gray @ Elsevier - The Three-legged Stool - Devops in the Enterprise

He sets the stage to discuss DevOps in the enterprise, contrasting it with the more fluid, collaborative culture of early-stage startups. As the company scales from 10 to 80 employees, Ian illustrates how DevOps principles erode under pressure — especially when sales teams push for rapid feature delivery, leading to a focus on functional requirements over automation, monitoring, or cross-functional collaboration. He candidly admits he dislikes the term 'DevOps' due to its misuse, and uses humor and personal anecdotes to underscore the tension between idealized DevOps ideals and the messy reality of enterprise growth.
quotes: I really, really hate the word [DevOps] because it's so misused.
You know, we've gone from being DevOps... in the blink of an eye.
Let's just get this out to the customer. We've got to do it.

title: Ali Aktar & Irfan Ansari @ ApplyingAgile - Its not all about Kubernetes
speaker: Unknown
summary: Ali Aqta, a DevOps practitioner with experience in Java, Groovy, and Scala, introduces a team focused on cloud and DevOps automation using HashiStack tools on Google Cloud Platform (GCP). The talk aims to demonstrate how to provision and automate fundamental infrastructure services—like DNS, security, compliance, and container scheduling—using tools such as Terraform, Vault, Nomad, and Consul. Aqta emphasizes the value of standardization, infrastructure-as-code, and DevOps principles, drawing parallels between HashiStack’s simplicity and Kubernetes’ complexity. He highlights HashiStack’s ecosystem as a cohesive, single-binary solution for cloud-native environments, suitable for both public clouds and on-premises. The session also touches on use cases like CI/CD pipeline integration with Jenkins, repository management with Nexus, and security through Vault, while acknowledging some unfinished demos (like Jenkins setup). The overall message is that HashiStack empowers teams to automate, standardize, and scale infrastructure efficiently without the overhead of managing complex systems like Kubernetes.

title: Ric Harvey - Technical Evangelist @ Amazon - From laptop to production with AWS Fargate
speaker: Ric Harvey

They highlight Fargate’s key advantage: eliminating the need for users to manage underlying infrastructure like EC2 instances. Instead, Fargate automatically provisions and scales containers based on compute requirements, with configurations ranging from 0.25 CPU to 4 virtual CPUs and up to 30GB RAM. The speaker emphasizes the platform’s ease of use, especially for teams already familiar with ECS (EC2 Container Service) and Kubernetes concepts like tasks and services. They also briefly touch on best practices for networking, such as using private subnets and NAT gateways for security. The talk concludes with a demo setup — a VPC with public and private subnets — to illustrate how Fargate integrates into real-world architectures. The speaker’s tone is engaging and slightly humorous, especially when addressing the audience’s participation and the 'trouble maker' in the front row.

We handle all that in the background for you.

title: Serhat Can - SWE @ OpsGenie - Key steps towards building a healthy on-call culture
speaker: Serhat Can
summary: Serthat from Obsini delivers a thoughtful talk on improving on-call practices in tech and beyond, emphasizing the human cost of constant alert fatigue. They begin by acknowledging the widespread, often-hated reality of on-call duties — affecting one in five Europeans, not just in IT. Serthat argues that while on-call is necessary, it must be made healthier, fairer, and more sustainable. They propose six steps to achieve this, beginning with transparency — communicating on-call expectations upfront during hiring or structural changes. They also stress defining clear responsibilities, including setting up roles like incident commanders and subject matter experts, and ensuring on-call staff aren’t expected to do feature work, but rather focus on improving monitoring and incident response. The speaker highlights the importance of fair scheduling, avoiding both over- and under-load, and respecting time zones to reduce stress. The talk is grounded in empathy and practical experience, aiming to transform on-call from a source of dread into a manageable, even empowering, part of operations.

It's very important because it helps you reduce stress and you distribute on-call.

title: Mika Boström - Head of Infrastr & Security @ Smarkets - Usability lessons

She positions herself as someone who understands high-stakes, always-on systems — gambling platforms are never offline, and downtime is costly. She acknowledges the tension between usability and security, and frames DevOps not as tooling, but as UX for engineers. Open Tracing is highlighted as a vendor-neutral, open standard that allows teams to instrument code deeply — even down to byte-level operations — without rewriting existing systems. Its language-agnostic nature and stable API make it a flexible choice for evolving platforms. title: Richard Marshall, Head of Platform @ Wealth Wizards: Life with kube, warts’n’all

After hiring a consultancy to kickstart engineering, they adopted microservices, containers, and Kubernetes — a move that simplified app logic for developers but transferred complexity to the platform layer. Richard highlights how CUBE has been instrumental in managing this new complexity, enabling rapid deployment, automation, and scalability without requiring teams to wrestle with Kubernetes operations. The core problem was a monolithic, poorly segmented network design that prevented proper isolation between environments (like staging and production), making it impossible to add new VPCs or split traffic without complex workarounds. The team ultimately redeployed the entire infrastructure over a couple of weeks, resulting in a cleaner, more scalable setup. title: Fun with SSL - Graeme Forbes

A core part of this system involved SSL negotiation and client certificate authentication, where Apache handled SSL termination and passed headers to Jenkins. The speaker also touches on the complexities of certificate revocation lists (CRLs), noting the tension between long certificate lifespans (which increase security risk) and frequent CRL updates (which are hard to maintain). They highlight the operational burden of keeping CRLs synchronized across all Apache instances without restarting services, which could break active user sessions. The talk is framed as a retrospective, with a touch of humor and frustration about the technical debt and systemic challenges of the time.

title: How to build a Docker Monitoring Solution- Alex Dias - DevOps at Outlyer
speaker: Unknown
summary: Alex, a senior developer at Altair, presents how the company built its Docker monitoring solution. The system uses an agent that can be deployed via Docker or as a process agent to collect metrics from containers and send them to a backend for visualization via dashboards. Alex notes that Docker adoption among customers has surged — from 0% to 12% in 18 months — and that customers now expect Docker-specific monitoring. The solution must handle significantly more metrics: an average host now generates eight times more metrics due to the presence of eight containers. Alex also highlights the challenge of container churn — containers often run for only two days on average (some as short as six minutes), which means the system must handle frequent re-identification of containers, leading to database scalability concerns. The core challenge lies in the isolation of containers, which makes data collection harder compared to VMs where processes are directly accessible. The team adapted their existing VM-based monitoring architecture to handle this new paradigm, avoiding the inefficiency of deploying agents inside each container.

The average host wants eight containers, so you have eight times more metrics per host.
